coredata.dc:title: Structure and properties of rapidly solidified Al–Cr–Fe–Ti–Si powder alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Bártová, B.
Vojtěch, D.
Verner, J.
Gemperle, A.
Studnička, V.
coredata.dc:description: In the present paper, microstructure, phase composition and elevated temperature behaviour of two rapidly solidified (RS) AlCr6Fe2Ti(0–1.5)Si1 (in wt.%) powder alloys are described. Rapidly solidified powders were composed of α(Al) solid solution matrix and finely dispersed intermetallic phases of spherical or irregular shapes. XRD and TEM investigations revealed the presence of quasi-crystalline icosahedral Al84.6Cr15.4, Al82Fe18, Al95Fe4Cr, Al74Cr20Si6 and crystalline Al, Al13Cr2, Al13Fe4, Al3FeSi phases. The fraction of quasi-crystalline phases in the form of spheroids was reduced as the powder particle size increased. The presence of titanium significantly increases the fraction of icosahedral phases. DTA measurement revealed an exothermic reaction at 450–500°C in the fine powder fraction (size 25–45μm) of the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 alloy. This fraction also exhibited a hardening after annealing at 350°C/50h. The hardening, as well as the exothermic effect were due to a decomposition of the icosahedral phases described by the reaction Al95Fe4Cr(Ti, Si) + Al84.6Cr15.4(Ti, Si) + Al74Cr20Si6(Fe, Ti) → Al13Cr2 + Al3FeSi + TiSi.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aluminium-based alloy
Rapid solidification
Quasi-crystalline phase
Powder metallurgy
Hardening

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Granulometric fractions of the powder alloys (a, AlCr6Fe2Si1; b, AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1).
Microstructures observed in the powder alloys (a, A type with equiaxed IPs; b, B type with irregular IPs; c, particle with eutectic colonies; d, particle with needle-like IPs).

Microstructures observed in the powder alloys as a function of powder particle size (a, AlCr6Fe2Si1; b, AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1; A, spheroids of IPs; B, irregular IPs; C, eutectic colonies or needle-like IPs).
IP interparticle spacing as a function of the powder particle size (a, AlCr6Fe2Si1; b, AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1).
DTA curves of both alloys recorded at heating rate of 5K/min (a, AlCr6Fe2Si1; b, AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1).
Hardness of the powder size 25–45μm (a) and 45–63μm (b) vs. annealing time at 350°C.
XRD patterns of various powder fractions of the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 alloy.
TEM image of an Al3FeSi particle and its diffraction pattern.
XRD patterns of various powder fractions of the AlCr6Fe2Si1 alloy.
XRD patterns of the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 alloy (powder size 25–45μm) in RS state and after 50 and 1200h of annealing at 350°C.
XRD patterns of the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 alloy (powder size 45–63μm) in RS state and after 312 and 750h of annealing at 350°C.
Chemical compositions of the investigated powder alloys (in wt.%)
Influence of powder particle size on the phase composition for the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 alloy (+, phase is found; −, phase is not found)
Influence of powder particle size on the phase composition for the AlCr6Fe2Si1 alloy (+, phase is found; −, phase is not found)

article.ce:title: Structure and properties of rapidly solidified Al–Cr–Fe–Ti–Si powder alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metals, Institute of Chemical Technology in Prague, Technická 5, 166 28 Prague 6, Czech Republic

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics ASCR, Na Slovance 2, 182 21 Prague 8, Czech Republic

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present paper, microstructure, phase composition and elevated temperature behaviour of two rapidly solidified (RS) AlCr6Fe2Ti(0–1.5)Si1 (in wt.%) powder alloys are described. Rapidly solidified powders were composed of α(Al) solid solution matrix and finely dispersed intermetallic phases of spherical or irregular shapes. XRD and TEM investigations revealed the presence of quasi-crystalline icosahedral Al84.6Cr15.4, Al82Fe18, Al95Fe4Cr, Al74Cr20Si6 and crystalline Al, Al13Cr2, Al13Fe4, Al3FeSi phases. The fraction of quasi-crystalline phases in the form of spheroids was reduced as the powder particle size increased. The presence of titanium significantly increases the fraction of icosahedral phases. DTA measurement revealed an exothermic reaction at 450–500°C in the fine powder fraction (size 25–45μm) of the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 alloy. This fraction also exhibited a hardening after annealing at 350°C/50h. The hardening, as well as the exothermic effect were due to a decomposition of the icosahedral phases described by the reaction Al95Fe4Cr(Ti, Si) + Al84.6Cr15.4(Ti, Si) + Al74Cr20Si6(Fe, Ti) → Al13Cr2 + Al3FeSi + TiSi.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past few decades a great effort has been devoted to the research of rapidly solidified (RS) aluminium alloys. Particularly, RS multi-component Al–TM (TM, transition metals) alloys have been studied extensively. The RS Al–TM alloys differ qualitatively from the commercially available Al-based alloys (e.g. Al–Cu–Mg, Al–Zn–Mg–Cu, Al–Si–Mg) showing moderate strength levels (up to 700MPa) and a low thermal stability. Until the discovery of the great potential of rapid solidification, the Al-based alloys had been considered to progressively approach their limits. RS can be realized by several procedures like melt spinning, melt atomisation, melt spraying, etc. These procedures enable alloying with transition metals in amounts much exceeding their equilibrium solubility in solid aluminium. Any rapidly solidified state is characterised by the following favourable microstructural features: supersaturation of solid solutions, fine-grained structure, amorphous phases either in part or in total volume of RS alloys, and nano-sized crystalline or quasi-crystalline phases [1]. After a compaction of the powder of a RS alloy, the semiproduct is obtained. Its properties can be varied in a wide range by changing the chemical composition, atomisation and compaction conditions. High-strength alloys with UTS higher than 1000MPa (e.g. Al–Ni–Ln) [1], high-ductility alloys (e.g. Al–Mn–Co) [2] and enhanced thermal stability alloys (e.g. Al–Fe–Cr–Ti) can be designed [3–10]. Some of the Al–TM based alloys, like Al–Fe–V–Si, Al–Fe–Ce, etc. have already been included into standard alloy systems [11].

The RS Al–Cr–TM (Fe, Ti, Zr, Mn, etc.) based alloys are representatives of the thermally stable alloys maintaining remarkably high UTS levels at temperatures higher than 300°C [3–10]. Their increased resistance to elevated temperatures may be attributed to much lower diffusion coefficients of the transition metals in solid aluminium in comparison with diffusion coefficients of common alloying elements like Cu and Mg [12]. Chromium exhibits an extended solid solubility (more than 6at.% compared to equilibrium solubility of 0.2at.%) in the RS state [13]. Hence, precipitation hardening may occur during high temperature compaction and/or during appropriate heat treatment.

The present work concentrates on rapidly solidified multi-component Al–Cr–Fe–Ti–Si powder alloys. Addition of silicon reduces the diffusion rate of chromium and promotes a hardening response during elevated temperature exposition [14]. Moreover, in some alloy systems like, e.g. Al–Fe–V–Si, silicon contributes to an increased strength and creep resistance by the formation of stable silicides [15,16].

The Al–Cr–Fe–Ti–Si alloys were prepared by melting of high purity (99.99%) aluminium with AlCr10, AlFe20, AlTi5 and AlSi30 master alloys in a resistance furnace. Afterwards, the alloys were melted again in an induction furnace in nitrogen atmosphere and then atomised by nitrogen at 450kPa pressure. Chemical compositions of the produced powders are shown in Table 1 .

The prepared powders were sieved to separate various granulometric fractions (<25, 25–45, 45–63, 63–100, 100–125 and >125μm). The fractions of particles smaller than 25μm and larger than 125μm were negligible (Fig. 1 ). Metallographic procedures (mounting, grinding and polishing) were used to characterize the microstructures in RS state. The thermal stability of the powders was checked by long-term (1200h) annealing at 350°C and measuring the hardness as a function of exposition time. Solid-state phase transformations were also monitored by DTA (heating rate 5K/min, standard Al2O3). In order to determine the phase composition in various stages of thermal exposition, XRD (analyser HUBER with X-ray source RIGAKU RU-300E and rotating anode) and TEM (JEOL JEM 1200 EX, 120kV) were employed. For TEM investigation, the α(Al) matrix of the powders was dissolved in a mixture containing 10g of iodine, 250g of methanol and 25g of tartaric acid. Extracted coarse intermetallic particles were characterized by electron diffraction.

The microstructures of both alloys AlCr6Fe2Si1 and AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 are very similar. They comprise particles of intermetallic phases (IPs), which are finely dispersed in the α(Al) matrix. The volume fractions of IPs were measured using an image analyser LUCIA. They form approximately 50% in all granulometric fractions. Segregation-free powder particles were not found even in the microstructures of the finest fractions. All particles contained IPs. The cooling rate was not sufficient to reach a metastable α(Al) liquidus in the peritectic Al–Cr diagram. The IPs rich in Cr and other elements solidify first. Similar results have been reported by Adkins and Tsakiropulos [7] who found a segregation-free microstructure only in very small particles of a few μm size. These authors suggested using a lower Cr concentration (below 3wt.%) to obtain featureless structure.

In addition to the dominating IP morphology, the powder particle size, i.e. the cooling rate, affects also the average IP particle spacing (Fig. 4 ). The spacing was measured in the dominating microstructures A and B. A higher cooling rate produces a smaller interparticle spacing. In smaller particles the cooling is more rapid, the undercooling is deeper and a larger number of possible nucleation sites become available. No significant difference between both alloys is observed concerning the interparticle spacings (Fig. 4a and b). Unlike in commonly produced Al-based wrought or cast alloys, titanium does not behave as a strong refining agent in the RS Al–Cr based alloys. The refining effect in common casting processes is known to be caused by finely dispersed inoculant particles [11]. In a rapid solidification process, the refining could result from a slow diffusivity of Ti in the melt. The formation of clusters of critical size in the melt becomes more difficult during rapid cooling when the slowly diffusing element is added. As a result, a deeper undercooling is achieved which could produce a finer microstructure. However, in our case the refining due to titanium is not observed.

Solid-state phase transformations in various fractions of both alloys were studied by DTA and the results are given in Fig. 5 . In the DTA records of the AlCr6Fe2Si1 alloy (Fig. 5a), no solid state reaction is observed. An almost constant slope in the whole temperature range characterizes the DTA curves. In contrast, the two fine powder fractions (size smaller than 25 and 25–45μm) of the Ti-containing alloy (Fig. 5b), exhibit an exothermic reaction in the temperature range of 450–500°C. The heat released in this reaction is higher for the powder size smaller than 25μm. For fractions with particle size larger than 45μm, the phase transformation is not detectable by DTA.

The nature of the phase transformation detected by DTA was revealed by XRD. Two possible solid state reactions may occur in crystalline or quasi-crystalline RS Al–TM based alloys and may explain the observed thermal effects. The first one is the precipitation of IPs from a supersaturated solid solution formed during rapid solidification. This precipitation occurs in a number of Al–TM alloys and is generally detected between 400 and 500°C. Under isothermal heating conditions, the precipitation may proceed at lower temperatures. The second reaction is a decomposition of a metastable phase different from the solid solution. The metastable phases are either crystalline or quasi-crystalline. The quasi-crystalline phases are sometimes denoted as Q-phases. The icosahedral Q-phases are characterised by quasi-periodic lattice and they have been observed in rapidly solidified Al–Mn [17,18], Al–Cr [19] or Al–V [20] alloys. The decomposition of these binary phases to crystalline phases starts at about 400–450°C. More complex phases of, e.g. Al–Fe–Cr–Ti system may decompose at significantly higher temperatures [10].

The behaviour of powders at elevated temperatures was followed also by hardness measurement during isothermal annealing at 350°C. The results for the powder particles of sizes between 25–45 and 45–63μm are shown in Fig. 6 . The plot of hardness shows that the highest hardness was measured for the RS state in the case of the AlCr6Fe2Si1 alloy. Annealing up to approximately 300h leads to a progressive reduction of hardness. Afterwards, the hardness remains almost constant up to 1200h of annealing. By comparing Fig. 6a and b, it can be seen that the hardness of this alloy practically does not depend on the powder particle size. The behaviour of the Ti-containing alloy is significantly different. An evident hardening of the fine fraction (Fig. 6a) compared to the original RS state was observed already after the first 50h of annealing. Then the hardness decreases slowly until the end of annealing. For the coarser fraction (Fig. 6b), the hardening response is significantly smaller. The hardness of the Ti-containing alloy (fraction 25–45μm) remains always higher than that of the alloy without Ti during the whole annealing time. It implies its increased thermal stability. In this work, the term thermal stability denotes the capability of an alloy to maintain a high hardness during a long-term annealing at elevated temperature.

The phase transformation detected at 450–500°C by DTA and the transformation accompanied by hardening detected during annealing at 350°C of the fine powder fraction of the Ti-containing alloy have evidently similar nature. The difference between the two temperatures can be attributed to a thermal hysteresis, which is generally observed when solid-state phase transformations occur under isothermal and anisothermal conditions.

Phase composition of the RS alloys was investigated by XRD and TEM. XRD patterns of various powder fractions of the Ti-containing alloy are shown in Fig. 7 . For the coarse fraction (100–125μm), the XRD measurement was complemented by TEM investigation of the IP particles separated from the α(Al) matrix. Besides the Al13Cr2 phase, TEM revealed also the presence of the Al3FeSi phase. Fig. 8 shows the TEM image of this phase and its diffraction pattern. The Al3FeSi phase was not detected by XRD probably due to its small volume fraction. The phase composition of the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 powder alloy is complex and it is summarized in Table 2 . In addition to the equilibrium crystalline phases (Al, Al13Cr2, Al13Fe4 and Al3FeSi), various quasi-crystalline icosahedral phases (Al84.6Cr15.4, Al82Fe18, Al95Fe4Cr and Al74Cr20Si6) were identified in the RS state. Surprisingly, XRD and TEM identified no Ti-containing phase. We assume that titanium is dissolved in the icosahedral phases and, perhaps, in other phases (e.g. α(Al) solid solution). This assumption is in agreement with data reported by Kimura et al. [4] who described various ternary Al–Fe–Cr–Ti icosahedral phases. Their results imply that the concentrations of Cr, Ti, Fe and Si in icosahedral phases can vary in a wide range.

As expected, the probability of the presence of metastable phases grows as the powder particle size decreases. This can be seen from the absence of the three quasi-crystalline phases (Al84.6Cr15.4, Al95Fe4Cr and Al74Cr20Si6) in the coarse fraction, as well as from the absence of the equilibrium Al13Fe4 phase in the fine powder fractions. At a high cooling rate, i.e. at a deep supercooling of the melt, the decreased diffusivity of the atoms results in a reduced order of the atoms in the clusters which are sites of future quasi-crystalline nuclei. The observed microstructures, see Figs. 2 and 3, reflect well the changes of phase composition with powder particle size. In fact, the spherical morphology is typical for the quasi-crystalline phase [2], so the spheroids occupy a large volume fraction of the fine powder particles. On the other hand, Fe-containing crystalline phases of irregular shape [22] are present mainly in the coarse fraction.

The phase composition of the other investigated alloy, i.e. AlCr6Fe2Si1, differs from the preceding one, see Fig. 9 and Table 3 . The dominating phases of all powder fractions are Al and Al13Cr2. The coarse fractions contain also the crystalline Al13Fe4 phase. The quasi-crystalline phases Al82Fe18, Al95Fe4Cr and Al74Cr20Si6 were identified in the RS AlCr6Fe2Si1 alloy. The icosahedral Al84.6Cr15.4 phase was not detected in contrast to the Ti-containing alloy. The presence of Ti, as a very slowly diffusing element, increases the volume fraction of quasi-crystalline phases in a powder.

Fig. 10 shows the XRD patterns of the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 alloy (powder fraction 25–45μm) in RS state, after 50 and 1200h of annealing at 350°C. These samples were investigated in detail to explain the observed hardening (Fig. 6a). The long-term annealing at 350°C leads to a decomposition of the icosahedral Al95Fe4Cr, Al84.6Cr15.4, Al82Fe18 and Al74Cr20Si6 phases and formation of the Al13Cr2, Al3FeSi and TiSi phases. In addition, these phases may have partially formed also by decomposition of the supersaturated α(Al) solid solution. However, the negligible deviation of the lattice parameter of the α(Al) solid solution from pure Al indicates that the supersaturation is small. From the XRD patterns it follows that the rate of decomposition of Al95Fe4Cr and Al84.6Cr15.4, is higher than that of Al74Cr20Si6 and Al82Fe18. The Al95Fe4Cr and Al84.6Cr15.4 phases disappeared during the first 50h of thermal exposition. The icosahedral phases Al74Cr20Si6 and Al82Fe18 were detected after further annealing. Although the exact chemical composition of the Al82Fe18 icosahedral phase is not given, as it is formed together with Al and Al3Fe in a rapidly solidified Al82Fe18 alloy [23], one can assume that this composition is not very far from that of the original alloy. Therefore, the relatively high contents of the slowly diffusing elements Fe and Cr in both the Al74Cr20Si6 and the Al82Fe18 phase may be the reason for their increased thermal stability.

The XRD patterns for the coarser fraction (45–63μm) of the Ti-containing alloy measured after 312 and 750h of annealing at 350°C are presented in Fig. 11 . Like in the preceding case, the icosahedral Al84.6Cr15.4 phase decomposes during annealing whereas the Al82Fe18 phase remains even after 750h of annealing. The products of phase transformations are similar, mainly Al13Cr2 and Al3FeSi. The TiSi phase was not detected. These results are in accordance with DTA (Fig. 5) and hardness (Fig. 6) measurements. A low volume fraction of the Al84.6Cr15.4 phase in the powder fraction of 45–63μm is the reason for the much lower thermal effect, as well as for the smaller hardening response.

The XRD measurements described above and those presented in Figs. 10 and 11 reveal the nature of the hardening observed in the fine powder fraction of the Ti-containing alloy at 350°C (Fig. 6a). The phase transformation that may be considered as the reason for this hardening is expressed by the following single equation:
(1)
A
l
95
F
e 4
Cr ( Ti , Si ) + A
l
84.6
C
r
15.4
( Ti , Si ) + A
l
74
C
r
20
S
i 6
( Fe , Ti ) → A
l
13
C
r 2
+ A
l 3
FeSi + TiSi

The icosahedral phases are generally hard, as dislocation movement is suppressed in a quasi-periodic lattice [10]. The maximum hardness is achieved when a structure contains the highest density of phase boundaries between the original phases and the products of their decomposition. This state probably corresponds to 50h of annealing at 350°C. Upon further annealing, the decomposition products coarsen and the hardness becomes reduced. Despite the relatively small content of Si in the alloy, the main part of hardening can be expected to result from the TiSi silicide particles, which are finely dispersed due to the low diffusivity of Ti in solid Al. In fact, silicides contribute significantly also to hardening of other RS Al–TM–Si alloys, e.g. Al–Fe–V–Si [15,16].

The results presented in this paper can be summarized as follows:
1.
The microstructures of the RS powders of the investigated Al–Cr–Fe–Ti–Si alloys are composed of the α(Al) solid solution matrix and dispersed intermetallic phases of spherical or irregular shapes. The volume fraction of the spheroids grows as the powder particle size decreases.
2.
The fine powder fraction (25–45μm) of the AlCr6Fe2Ti1.5Si1 alloy exhibits an exothermic reaction at 450–500°C. The hardness measurement of this powder fraction reveals a hardening after 350°C/50h annealing. Neither hardening nor thermal effect is observed for the AlCr6Fe2Si1 alloy.
3.
XRD and TEM investigations of the RS Al–Cr–Fe–Ti–Si alloys reveal the presence of quasi-crystalline icosahedral Al84.6Cr15.4, Al82Fe18, Al95Fe4Cr, Al74Cr20Si6 and crystalline Al, Al13Cr2, Al13Fe4, Al3FeSi phases. The number and volume fraction of the quasi-crystalline phases decrease, as the powder particle size increases and as the titanium content decreases.
4.
During annealing of the Ti-containing alloy at 350°C, the icosahedral phases decompose, according to the following equation:
A
l
95
F
e 4
Cr ( Ti , Si ) + A
l
84.6
C
r
15.4
( Ti , Si ) + A
l
74
C
r
20
S
i 6
( Fe , Ti ) → A
l
13
C
r 2
+ A
l 3
FeSi + TiSi
This decomposition is responsible for the hardening (350°C/50h) and for the exothermic effect monitored by DTA.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research on rapidly solidified Al-based alloys was financially supported by the research project MSM 223100002 and also by the Grant Agency of the Czech Republic (project number: 106/00/0571).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: ASM Specialty Handbook, Aluminium and Aluminium Alloys, ASM International, 1993.
